text: Interval training improves performance in part by enhancing the capacity for aerobic energy provision, even in individuals who are already trained.

Two primary mechanisms by which interval training enhances aerobic capacity are increased whole-body maximal oxygen uptake (VO2max) and enhanced oxidative metabolism in skeletal muscle due to increased mitochondrial content.
Interval training can elicit superior physiological responses compared to moderate-intensity continuous exercise when total work is matched.
Interval training can produce similar physiological adaptations to moderate-intensity continuous exercise despite a reduced training volume and time commitment.

Physiological responses to interval training depend on the specific nature of the intervention (intensity, duration, number of intervals, recovery periods) and the initial training state of the individual.
